coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent color analysis of ascorbic acid by ratiometric fluorescent paper utilizing hybrid carbon dots-silica coated quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Tingting
Zhu, Chen
Xu, Shuai
Wu, Xinghan
Zhang, Xiaotong
Zheng, Yurong
Wu, Mengjiao
Tong, Zhiwei
Fang, Weijun
Zhang, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring the concentration of ascorbic acid (AA) in biological samples is essential to food quality and healthcare. Various fluorescence methods widely reported for the detection of AA are usually based on “turn-on” or “turn-off” principle. However, few ratiometric fluorescent sensors were reported for AA determination. Additionally, the application of ratiometric fluorescence probe is usually limited by the narrow range of color changes and insensitivity color change of the AA concentration. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescent test paper to achieve the continuous color changes from red to blue was developed for the fluorometric qualitative and quantitative determination of AA. The nanoprobe consists of blue-emitting carbon dots (C-dots) and red-emitting silica coated quantum dots (QDs@SiO2) with certain ratiometric fluorescence intensity of blue to red. The fluorescence of blue-emitting CDs (peaking at 450 nm) is quenched by Fe3+, then the blue fluorescence is restored consecutively with the gradual increasing of AA concentration due to the reduction from Fe3+ to Fe2+ by using AA. While the fluorescence of red-emitting QD@SiO2 (peaking at 630 nm) works as a stable internal standard. Therefore, a distinguish color transition from red to blue can be observed by naked eyes intuitively with the increasing of AA concentration. By calculating the ratiometric fluorescent intensity at 450 nm and 630 nm or observing the color of the mixture solution by naked eyes, the concentration of AA can be detected easily and accurately. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent intensity ratio (I450/I630) shows linear relationship with the concentration of AA from 0 to 70 μM with the detection limit as low as 3.17 μM. Finally, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor paper was further developed for AA detection with satisfactory results in fruit juice. The method proposed here gives the wide applications of fluorescent test paper in food and biological assays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence probe
CDs
QD@SiO2
Ascorbic acid level detection
Electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent spectra of (a) CDs, (b) QD@SiO2, (c) CDs-QD@SiO2. The inset shows the corresponding fluorescence photos under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) The Fluorescent spectra of the ratiometric fluorescence probe with the different concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 150 μM. (B) The Fluorescent spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution with different concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 190 μM. The insets show the corresponding fluorescent photos under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) The selectivity of CD-QD@SiO2 to various metal ions (150 μM). The insert photos were taken under 365 nm UV lamp. The I450/I630 ratio is the fluorescence intensity ratio of CD to QDs@SiO2. (B) The anti-interference experiment of CD-QD@SiO2 in the presence of various metal ions. The black columns represent the I450/I630 with addition of various interfering ions (150 μM). The striped columns represent the I450/I630 after the further addition of Fe3+ (150 μM) into the solution with these interfering ions.

(A) The Fluorescent spectra of the CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ fluorescent probe with the different concentration of AA from 0 to 150 μM. (B) The Fluorescent spectra of the CDs-Fe3+ in aqueous solution with different concentration of AA from 0 to 190 μM. The insets show the corresponding fluorescent photos under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The PL intensity ratio (I450/I630) of the CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ probe towards different concentration of AA. The inset shows the linearity of the CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ probe towards different concentration of AA.

(A) The selectivity of CD-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ to various compounds (150 μM). The insert photos were taken under 365 nm UV lamp. The I450/I630 ratio is the fluorescence intensity ratio of CD to QDs@SiO2. (B) The anti-interference experiment of CD-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ in the presence of various compounds. The striped columns represent the I450/I630 with addition of various interfering compounds (150 μM). The black columns represent the I450/I630 after the further addition of AA (150 μM) into the solution with these interfering compounds.

Visualization of AA using the fluorescent test papers prepared by printing CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ ink onto a piece of filter paper. Visual detection of AA in kiwi juice. The photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Two typical color proportions of blue to red in a single dual-emissive fluorescent probe and resultant color-varying ranges in a ratiometric fluorescent sensor: (A) 1:1 dual-color probe (B) 6:1 dual-color probe. The white arrows indicate the ranges of color variation.
Schematic illustration of the formation of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe and the visual detection principle for AA.
Comparison of recently reported methods based on nano-materials for the detection of AA.
AA content qualification in fresh kiwi juice.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a: kiwi juice, b, c: kiwi juice of a was added with 10 μM AA twice.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent color analysis of ascorbic acid by ratiometric fluorescent paper utilizing hybrid carbon dots-silica coated quantum dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring the concentration of ascorbic acid (AA) in biological samples is essential to food quality and healthcare. Various fluorescence methods widely reported for the detection of AA are usually based on “turn-on” or “turn-off” principle. However, few ratiometric fluorescent sensors were reported for AA determination. Additionally, the application of ratiometric fluorescence probe is usually limited by the narrow range of color changes and insensitivity color change of the AA concentration. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescent test paper to achieve the continuous color changes from red to blue was developed for the fluorometric qualitative and quantitative determination of AA. The nanoprobe consists of blue-emitting carbon dots (C-dots) and red-emitting silica coated quantum dots (QDs@SiO2) with certain ratiometric fluorescence intensity of blue to red. The fluorescence of blue-emitting CDs (peaking at 450 nm) is quenched by Fe3+, then the blue fluorescence is restored consecutively with the gradual increasing of AA concentration due to the reduction from Fe3+ to Fe2+ by using AA. While the fluorescence of red-emitting QD@SiO2 (peaking at 630 nm) works as a stable internal standard. Therefore, a distinguish color transition from red to blue can be observed by naked eyes intuitively with the increasing of AA concentration. By calculating the ratiometric fluorescent intensity at 450 nm and 630 nm or observing the color of the mixture solution by naked eyes, the concentration of AA can be detected easily and accurately. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent intensity ratio (I450/I630) shows linear relationship with the concentration of AA from 0 to 70 μM with the detection limit as low as 3.17 μM. Finally, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor paper was further developed for AA detection with satisfactory results in fruit juice. The method proposed here gives the wide applications of fluorescent test paper in food and biological assays.

ce:section.ce:para:
Ascorbic acid (AA), more well-known as vitamin C, has been widely used in food, chemical, nutraceutical, pharmaceutical and cosmetic fields.[
1
] AA is a powerful antioxidant used to reduce the oxidative stress of peroxidase. AA functions as a key coenzyme in many metabolic pathways and plays an important role in human life. Many important biosynthesis processes also need the involving of AA. The intakes of AA are related to the states of people's health. For example, the deficiency of AA in body will result in scurvy, anemia, and decrease in immunity.[
2–4
] However, excessive intakes of AA also lead to urinary stone, diarrhea and stomach convulsion. 5
,
6 Therefore, the measurement of the content of AA in food and biological sample has great significance for food quality and healthcare.

Ascorbic acid, iron (III) chlorideanhydrous, citric acid, ethylenediamine, ethanol, ammonium hydroxide, tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), Te powder, NaBH4, CdCl2·5H2O, 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), NaOH and H2SO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Kiwi fruits were purchased from a supermarket.

Fluorescent spectra were determined by cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. AJEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope was utilized to observe shapes of CDs and QD@SiO2. Fluorescent photographs were taken under an AGL-9406 UV lamp with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)

ce:section.ce:para: 2 mL of CDs and 6 mL QD@SiO2 with the fluorescence intensity ratio of 6:1 was injected into the vacant cartridge. The “ink” was printed on the filter paper through an inkjet printer connected to a computer and the printing process was repeated several times to enhance the amount of ratiometric fluorescent probe. The obtained filter paper displayed strong blue fluorescence color under a 365 nm UV lamp.

AA detection in aqueous solution: 20 μL of CDs and 60 μL of QD@SiO2 were added into 2 mL of ultrapure water. After adding 150 μM Fe3+ into above solution to quench the blue fluorescence of CD, different concentration of AA were mixed into the above solution and reacted for 30 min at room temperature. The PL spectra of the mixture solution were measured upon being excited at 360 nm by a fluorescence spectrometer.

Detection of AA in kiwi juice on test paper: The kiwi fruit mixed with 30 mL ultrapure water was squeezed by a juice extractor firstly. Then the mixture was filtered to remove any solid suspensions by using a qualitative filter paper. Small amount of kiwi juice was diluted and dropped onto the as-prepared test paper with CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ nanoprobe. The color changes of test paper were observed under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The available ranges of color variation are usually governed under the red-green-blue (RGB) principle. The color choice and fluorescence intensity ratio of each composed probe are the significant factors affecting the mixed wide color variation probe. (Scheme 1 ). The width of the color variation of the mixed probe in the detection of the analyte is in direct proportion to the distance of the two colors of composed probe separated on the chromaticity. In our ratiometric probe, the color of CDs (blue fluorescence) and QD@SiO2 (red fluorescence) on chromaticity board exhibit relatively long distance. Additionally, the beginning color of the mixed ratiometric probe depends on the ratio of fluorescence intensity of each composed probe. The closer the beginning color of the ratiometric probe is to one of composed probe color, the wider the color change of the ratiometric probe in the detection of the analyte. An intermediate composite purple will result from the composite proportion of 1:1 blue/red in fluorescent intensity and thus the maximal range of color variations is either “from purple to red” or “from purple to blue” by fluorescent response to an analyte, as indicated with white arrow in Scheme 1A. The formation of intermediate composite color greatly suppresses the theoretical color-varying range of a dual-color probe, which makes the wider range “from blue to red” or “from red to blue” not achieved. When the proportion of blue to red is adjusted to 6:1 (Scheme 1B), the probe still displays a blue color, avoiding the formation of intermediate purple. In the presence of analyte, the blue fluorescence of CDs was quenched by analyte and the ratiometric probe of CDs-QD@SiO2 displays a wide multicolor variation from blue to blue purple to purple to pink to red, which will extend the color-varying range in colorimetric assays.

The morphology and structure of CDs and QD@SiO2 were characterized by various analysis methods. The TEM imaging of CDs in Figure S1A shows the size of ~2 nm, and QDs@SiO2 exhibit the size of ~110 ± 10 nm (Figure S1B). The surface groups of CDs were investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in Figure S2, the element survey scan for CDs exhibited C, N and O at 285, 400 and 531 eV, respectively. The C1s peak was further deconvoluted to identify the different bonding states of C specie. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s of CDs revealed three types of carbon atoms: aliphatic (CC and C–C) (284.55 eV), oxygenated (285.84 eV) and nitrous (287.14 eV). These Gaussian peaks of carbon identified in the CDs have been previously observed in carbon dots [27]. The blue fluorescence of the prepared CDs centered at 450 nm, could be quenched by Fe3+. The emission spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ display little spectral overlap (Fig. S3A), thus the resonance energy transfer (FRET) should not be the dominant pathway. The fluorescence quenching may be attributed to the non-radiative electron-transfer and explained as below. Owing to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs, CDs could capture Fe3+ due to the formation of coordination bond [27]. Additionally, the electrons in the excited state of CDs will transfer to the half-filled d orbits of Fe3+ (4s23 d5), resulting in the blue fluorescence quenching of CDs with the increasing of Fe3+ concentration. To further investigate fluorescence quenching mechanism, zeta potential, dynamic light scattering and time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) of CDs and CDs-Fe3+ were measured. The pure CDs displayed negative charges as manifested by their negative zeta potentials (−3.67 mV). After binding with Fe3+, zeta potential of CDs-Fe3+ increased from −3.67 to 14.4 mV (Figure S3C), which indicated successful coordination of positively charged Fe3+ onto the negatively charged CDs surface. DLS experiments showed that the average size distribution of CDs was ~3.1 nm (Figure S3D), which is consistent with the size distribution obtained by TEM imaging. A size of ~4.4 nm was obtained for CD-Fe3+, which reflects an increase in size upon the binding of Fe3+ ions. For TCSPC, the decay time of CDs is 3.52 ns? After coordination with Fe3+, the decay time of CDs-Fe3+ decreased to 1.25 ns(Figure S3B). The greatly reduced lifetime demonstrate the fast electron transfer process was occurred in CDs-Fe3+, and resulting in the dynamic quenching of the fluorescence of CDs. On the other hand, due to antioxidant and reducing properties of AA, it could easily bind the positively charged Fe3+ and reduce the oxidation state of the Fe3+ from +3 to +2, resulting in the disruption of the complexation between CDs and Fe3+ as well as the restoration of CDs fluorescence. Therefore, the CDs-QD@SiO2 ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed by blue emission of CDs and red emission of QD@SiO2 (Scheme 2 ). CDs act as a recognition site to Fe3+ and AA, leading to the blue fluorescence quenching and restoring respectively. However, the other component of QD@SiO2 in the ratiometric nanoprobe serves as a reliable reference signal due to various metal ions have no influence on the fluorescence of QD@SiO2 (Figure S4). The quenching and restoring of blue fluorescence of CDs and the stability of red fluorescence of QDs@SiO2 cause a gradual color evolution from red to blue for the visualization assay of AA.

As demonstrated in Fig. 1 , the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe possesses two emission peaks centered at 450 nm and 630 nm corresponding to the emission of CDs and QD@SiO2. Under a UV lamp irradiation, pure CDs and QD@SiO2 solutions emit strong blue and red fluorescence respectively, and the mixture with ratio of 6:1 (blue to red) displays blue fluorescence. Injecting QD@SiO2 into CDs to fabricate the ratiometric fluorescence probe, there are no obvious influence on the fluorescence intensity of CDs and QD@SiO2, indicating that no FRET occurs between CDs and QD@SiO2 and further confirming that QDs were successfully embedded into SiO2 NPs. To check whether the dual-emission fluorescence probe can be used in the system, the fluorescence stability of the ratiometric probe was studied by continuously exposed to 365 nm UV lamp from 0 to 60 min with 5 min interval. The fluorescence intensity of probes at 450 nm and 630 nm remained unchanged, indicating the excellent photostability of CDs, QD@SiO2 and CDs-QD@SiO2 (Figure S5). Time dependent fluorescence of ratiometric probe were investigated by the addition of Fe3+ and AA (Figure S6). In the “OFF” step, 150 μM Fe3+ was added into the CDs-QD@SiO2 system, and the PL intensity of CDs-QD@SiO2/Fe3+ was stable within 1 min. The quenching process happens quickly without any waiting. Therefore, an incubation time of 1 min is sufficient for quenching. During the “ON” step, CDs- QD@SiO2/Fe3+ nanoprobe hybrid was mixed with 150 μM AA, and Figure S6D depicted that the fluorescence ratio (I450/I630) continued to rise with the increasing of time, but the change of the ratio was almost negligible after 30 min. To achieve a quick response, 30 min of incubation time was confirmed for further AA detection.

In order to construct a ratiometric probe, fluorescent response to Fe3+ of individual CDs with blue emission and QD@SiO2 with red emission were studied. Adding Fe3+ with the concentration from 0 to 190 μM, the blue fluorescence of CDs centered at 450 nm were steadily decreased (Fig. 2 B). The inserted photographs displayed the faded blue fluorescence intensity under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. In contrast, the red fluorescence of QD@SiO2 kept constant with the addition of Fe3+ from 0 to 150 μM (Figure S7A) and the insert picture of red fluorescence intensity kept stable with the addition of Fe3+ under UV lamp. Thus, blue emission of CDs could act as signal unit and red emission of QD@SiO2 as internal standard unit to assemble ratiometric fluorescence probe. By optimizing the fluorescence ratio of blue to red, the blue fluorescence of CDs to red fluorescence of QD@SiO2 with fluorescence intensity ratio of 6:1 in ratiometric probe was chosen to construct ratiometric probe, and it exhibits the maximum broad color evolution from blue to red under UV lamp irradiation. As shown in Fig. 2A, in the presence of Fe3+ from 0 to 150 μM, the fluorescence peak centered at 450 nm was consecutively drop from the intensity of 228 to the intensity of 25, while the red emission peak centered at 630 nm remains unchanged under the same condition, demonstrating the electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+. Further increase the concentration of Fe3+ beyond 150 μM, the decreased slope became flat. The mixture solution exprienced rich color evolutions from blue to purple to purplish red to pink to red (Fig. 2A , Insert picture).

The selectivity of CD-QD@SiO2 to Fe3+ was investigated, and various metal ions, such as Cr6+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, K+, Al3+ and Ca2+, were selected for experimental comparison. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity I450/I630 of ratiometric probe was greatly quenched by Fe3+ with the concentration of 150 μM, while the fluorescence intensity of probe in the presence of other metal ions kept constant under the same concentration. Under the irradiation of UV lamp with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, only the solution with Fe3+ exhibited red fluorescence, while the other metal ions still show blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the anti-interference ability of CD-QD@SiO2 was also evaluated (Fig. 3B, Figure S9). The CD-QD@SiO2 probe will not be interfered by coexisting metal ions. The experiments demonstrate that CD-QD@SiO2 probe has excellent selectivity to Fe3+.

It is well known that AA has a strong reducing ability, the capability of CDs-Fe3+, QD@SiO2–Fe3+ and CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ system as nanosensors for AA assay were investigated. The fluorescent peak of CDs-Fe3+ recovered continuously with the addition of AA from 0 to 190 μM in Fig. 4 B. Thus the fluorescent color of CDs changed from dark to bright blue distinctly. The recovery of fluorescent intensity of CDs can be attributed to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ by AA addition, which destroys the electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+. On the contrary, the fluorescence of red emitting QD@SiO2–Fe3+ peaking at 630 nm has on obvious change when adding the same amount of AA. The insert shows the red fluorescent intensity of QD@SiO2–Fe3+ stayed constant under a 365 nm UV lamp (Figure S7B). The ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe of blue CDs and red QD@SiO2 with a ratio of 6:1 was applied to visually measure the concentration of AA. As shown in Fig. 4A, with 150 μM Fe3+ injected into the fluorescent nanoprobe solution, blue emission peaking at 450 nm of CDs was quenched efficiently while the red emission peaking at 630 nm of QD@SiO2 kept completely constant. Particularly worth mentioning is that the CDs nearly have been flatten after adding of Fe3+, resulting in the fading of blue emission and unveiling of red emission. After AA was mixed with the nanoprobe and incubated for 30 min, the fluorescence emission of CDs at 450 nm gradually increased with the increase of AA concentration while the fluorescence emission of QD@SiO2 at 630 nm had no changes. It's worth noting that distinct color variation of the system from red to pink to purple to blue purple to blue was observed clearly by the naked eyes under a 365 nm UV lamp, ranging from 0 to 150 μM AA. The ratiometric fluorescence method we proposed contributes to estimating the content of AA in food or biological sample by naked eyes intuitively. Fig. 5 showed there was a good linear relationship between I450/I630 and the concentration of AA in the range of 0–70 μM. The linear regression equation was I450/I630 = 1.0158 + 0.0471CAA (μM) with the detection limit of 3.17 μM for AA by 3σ rule. A comparison of materials, linear ranges, detection limits and methods between other methods and this methods for AA detection reported previously was listed in Table 1 . Compared to previously reported methods for quantitative determination of AA, the present assay system we established not only offered a comparable or superior linear range and detection limit, but more intuitive by naked eyes.

To evaluate the selectivity of the method we proposed, potential interference of other compounds were investigated with the same concentration of 150 μM. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity I450/I630 of ratiometric probe was restored remarkably when AA was added with the concentration of 150 μM, while the I450/I630 value of probe in the presence of other compounds kept constant under the same condition. Under the UV lamp irradiation of 365 nm, only the solution with AA showed blue fluorescence, while the remaining compounds and metal ions still exhibit red fluorescence. The anti-interference ability of CD-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ in the presence of a variety of compounds and metal ions were also evaluated (Fig. 6B, S10). In addition, the CD-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ probe doesn't influenced by the coexistence of other compounds and metal ions. The results mentioned above illustrated that most of compounds and metal ions almost have no influence on our system and AA detection can be performed with high selectivity.

The ratiometric fluorescent probe were investigated for their practical application in AA detection in fruit juice. The kiwi fruit was first mixed with 30 mL ultrapure water and squeezed by a juice extractor, followed by the filtration to remove any solid suspensions. The concentration of AA in kiwi juice was calculated according to the previous publication [32]. By using our ratiometric fluorescent probe, the concentration of AA in kiwi juice was measured to be 29.7 μM (Table 2 ). The possibility of using the proposed method for the analysis of real samples was tested by determining the recovery of known amounts of AA added to the kiwi juice. After spiking 10 μM AA twice into the above juice sample, the contents of AA were found to be 39.3 μM and 48.9 μM with the relative deviations of −2.2% and −2.6%. As presented in Table 2, recoveries ranging from 97.4% to 98.3% were observed during the experiment proposing the method was feasible for AA detection in real fruit sample.

According to the above strategy, a fluorescent test paper for the visual semi-quantification detection of AA has been prepared by printing CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ nanohybrid onto a piece of filter paper which were mixed by CD and QD@SiO2 with fluorescence intensity ratio of 6:1 and further added 150 μM Fe3+. The test paper with CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ fluorescent ink displayed a red fluorescence under the irradiation of a UV lamp at 365 nm. The capability of fluorescent paper to determine AA in aqueous solution by direct observation of colors on test paper under UV lamp were tested. Dropping the solution of AA with different concentration, the fluorescent paper exhibits consecutive color change from red to pink to purplish red to purple to blue fluorescence with the increasing of AA concentration from 0 to 150 μM (Fig. 7 ), further suggesting that the color evolution on the fluorescence test paper is due to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ by AA addition and resulting in the restoring of the blue fluorescence of CD. As shown in Fig. 6, 2
2 "As shown in Fig.6, the color change from red to red purple…" should be "As shown in Fig. 7, the color change from red to red purple…" the color change from red to red purple were clearly discerned even at the AA concentration as low as 10 μM. Each dosage interval of 10 μM could be easily distinguished by naked eyes. Therefore, the fluorescent test papers possess the ability of semiquantitative determination of AA concentration in solution.

The applicability of the fluorescent test papers for the detection of AA in fruit juice were further examined. Certain amount of kiwi juice and further spiked with AA twice at the concentrations of 10 μM were added onto the fluorescent test papers. The fluorescent test papers showed the obvious color responses to the different AA concentrations. The color evolution tendency for AA detection in kiwi juice is similar to that in aqueous solution (Fig. 7). All the results demonstrated that our fluorescent test papers exhibit high accuracy and excellent visual effect which can meet the requirements of the visual detection of AA in fruit juice.

In summary, we have successfully designed a ratiometric fluorescent probe containing CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ hybrid to semiquantitative assay of AA level by the change of ratiometric fluorescence and the discernible color responses with the dosage of AA. The CDs-QD@SiO2–Fe3+ hybrid consisted of blue-emitting CDs, red-emitting QD@SiO2 and Fe3+ with the fluorescence intensity ratio of 6:1 and the concentration of 150 μM, in which the ratiometric fluorescence of CDs-QD@SiO2 was firstly quenched by Fe3+ via electron transfer from CD to Fe3+, and subsequently the blue emission of CDs was restored by the reduction of AA from Fe3+ to Fe2+, inducing a dosage-sensitive consecutive wide-colored evolution from blue to red. With this strategy, AA could be specifically detected over a range from 0 to 70 μM with a detection limit 3.17 μM. Compared with previous reports for the detection of AA, the advantages of our probe in this work are listed as below. Firstly, the dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescence achieves a dosage-sensitive consecutive color variation for the clear visualization of AA. Secondly, the high-quality ratiometric fluorescent test paper shows a satisfying performance for the detection of AA in real samples, which can give a clear visual judgment of AA level even as low as 10 μM. Therefore, our design of ratiometric fluorescent test paper provides a sensitive and convenient way of detecting AA in food industry.

Tingting Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Chen Zhu: Methodology. Shuai Xu: Methodology. Xinghan Wu: Investigation. Xiaotong Zhang: Investigation. Yurong Zheng: Investigation. Mengjiao Wu: Validation. Zhiwei Tong: Validation. Weijun Fang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Kui Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors state that there are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21703255), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (No.1908085MB47, 1708085MB35), the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Anhui Medical University (No.XJ201808), Anhui University Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Education Training Program (No.S201910366073) and “Early Contact Research" Training Program for Clinical Medical Students (No. 2018-ZQKY-04, 2019-ZQKY-21) of Anhui Medical University.

Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-oxidation reduction strategy for highly selective detection of ascorbic acid with dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric signals
Sensor Actuator B Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen H, Liu Y, Li H, Zhang Y, Yao S. Non-oxidation reduction strategy for highly selective detection of ascorbic acid with dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric signals. Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem, 2019;281:983-988.

Oncol. Lett
